data.title: Eastman Chemical: Went 'Buy' Too Early, But Now It's Cheap
data.description:
Sometimes you go into companies at a price that you later end up regretting quite a bit. Not because it's a bad company, per se, but because everything you do as a value investor depends on the value of the thing you're buying when you buy it. This is a relevant discussion because the company I am speaking of here, Eastman Chemicals (EMN), is not a bad company. It's just a company that's been under recent strain and pressure due to results and macro, which, unfortunately, has led it to underperform the market.

I bought shares in accordance with my latest recommendation, rating, and article for the business, which you can find here.

Eastman Chemicals is an attractive business from a fundamental perspective. A business could be little else when it has a BBB, IG-rated credit, and a dividend yield of about 4.5% that, by all appearances, is well-covered even during an earnings slump (which the company in 2025-2026 seems to be going through). So let's see what the company offers us, what we might do here.

EMN is a specialty chemicals company - not a commodity chemicals company. That means that the company's end-markets and producers focus on high-value and low-volume products (or formulations, in chemical vernacular) for highly specific applications. It insulates the company from broader commodity cycles but also increases the volatility when the specific specialties the company works with are in lower demand.

An example of a specialty chemical is something for pharma or an additive. An example of a commodity chemical, for comparison, is sulfuric acid. One is produced in bulk, the other in much lower quantities.

Eastman Chemical - Upside is Now Better, but Growth is Moderated

The drivers and what makes a company profitable in one versus the other are two entirely different things - in a commodity, it's all about scale and cost leadership, such as BASF (BASFY). I like commodity players as well as specialty players. In specialty, it's all about the R&D side, the service, and attractive patents.

What makes Eastman appealing is its leadership in many fields in sustainable materials, such as molecular recycling. It, over the years, has moved from a traditional supplier of chemicals to a specialty chemicals leader with high margins. It's not a unique move - many commodity players are looking to or have made similar moves because they want the advantages of either a combined operation or the high margins in the specialty field.

What really makes Eastman different is the specialization of molecular recycling to handle the problem of plastic waste. Eastman has some of the largest plants in the world for this - in TS and TX, as well as France. EMN is thus a global player in an attractive and forward-oriented field. These projects (which are not yet completely done) are expected to drive EBITDA and EPS growth going forward - though not until 2029.

The technologies they use for this, such as polyester and carbon renewal, are proven to break down complex plastics into materials completely similar to fossil-based things, which means that the output is "not dangerous" as plastics anymore - instead, virgin plastics, which do not degrade and can therefore be used more effectively.

Aside from its recycling ambitions, Eastman also has an attractive specialty materials operation. It has patents in Tritan (BPA-free material with consumer applications) and other advanced materials and functional products, including even textiles like Naia Renew, which, based on recent results, seems to be growing in fashion.

Recent results for the company show us the advantages of the company, in my view, and how it's being undervalued. This is despite the fact that 2025 was objectively a year of EPS decline.

EMN managed to generate in excess of $900M, close to a billion dollars, in OCF. This shows cash generation. It also exceeded its cost reduction plan; it achieved the operational goals for its recycling plant in TS with good production, and its products are gaining more and more traction. It also returned cash to shareholders using both dividends and buybacks.

Unfortunately, none of this could prevent an operating loss and EPS decline, as well as the general and macro-related price-cost trends. EBIT margin didn't just fall; it crashed by almost 700 bps YoY, and the lower sales driven by issues in inventory on the customer side also affected the company. Revenue was down 10-12% YoY for 4Q and 7-10% for the full year.

However, a small consideration here. The company's trends were not down due to ineffective or lower pricing. The company is getting the same money (almost) for the products it sells. And since much of the volume decline can be laid at the feet of macro trends, I view this as less significant than some analysts for the long-term company prospects. In fact, in the additives segment, the company managed to increase its revenues using pricing, which lends credence to the assumption that EMN is in a good position in its market.

For 2026, the company is ratcheting up its cost savings targets, as well as CapEx targets, now that much of the initial investment in its initiatives is finished. I also note that there were significant shutdowns of assets in 2025 - without those, results should be at the very least $30M higher just on the basis of that. The current OCF expectation for 2026 is in line with 2025. The current 1Q26 EPS, which I believe to be valid, is about $1.2/share on the high end, which, on the basis of 2026, comes to an AEPS forecast of about $5.7/share when accounting for the seasonal weighting of its annual EPS. This not only covers the $3.33/share dividend with a very high safety, because the company does not have much debt, but also risks for interest rate increases are lower than in other companies.

2025 was not a good year because Eastman is still waiting to see much of the upside from its operations that it started up. The company is underperforming due to poor trends in end markets it cannot itself affect - like automotive, construction, and industrial - coupled with significant destocking on the consumer inventory side.

The combination of cost reductions together with more contributions from recycling should slowly start to turn this around - which is also the expectation we currently have at about 5-7% AEPS growth for the 2026E period. The fact that even during one of the worst macro markets in a long time, the company still saw hundreds of millions in FCF is another reason for upside here.

The positive outlook for the first quarter in 2026 generally confirms this. With the company's balance sheet safe, I don't see much risk to the near term at this valuation. The strong cash flow (FCF) is enough in itself to cover the dividend, and debt is below 3.3x in terms of net debt/EBITDA.

Valuation for the Company - Some Near-Term Risks Remain, but Overall the Case is Strong

There are a few moving parts in the equation that justify attention from analysis. First of all, the company's 2026 Q1 and 2026 FY assumptions had a Brent crude price of about $70/bbl on the high end. As a feedstock, higher oil prices result in higher expenses. Obviously, the oil price is causing increased costs, but I would consider this to abate over the 2nd quarter of this year (I consider the war likely to dial down within 1-2 months).

The company has guided for further buybacks this year. I do not consider this to be an efficient use of capital at this particular time, given the company's valuation at about 13.5x normalized (P/E), which is already above the 20-year average of 12-13x.

In my last article, I gave the company a PT of $110/share. I am slightly lowering this to reflect the pressure of near-term uncertainties, to about $104/share. That means that if bought at below $104/share, there is an upside to a 12-14x P/E, which I consider to be a good approximation of the company's long-term value. I argue this growth (about 12-13% per year) is likely because of the company's operations in recycling coupled with a fundamental recovery in the more traditional sectors, such as specialty materials, additives, and fibers—with Fibers especially having a turnaround potential due to the low performance in 2025.

History has shown us that EMN tends to go up, then down. This reflects its underlying cyclicality and that of the sector overall.

Tendentially, it's a good idea to buy the company cheaply - and in this case, I believe the company is likely to see a longer-term growth trend exactly due to the recycling segment, which I believe has significant potential. Eastman is capturing large-scale demand from players with significant sustainability goals, given the capacity the company's assets have - well in excess of hundreds of millions of pounds per year.

The technology that Eastman uses not only does not even compete with other recyclers, but it also has a yield of over 90%, and the technique can handle the plastics that other methods can't touch - such as carpets, polyester-based textiles, and the like. The current facilities are just the flagship assets - the coming potential for expansion of this idea is in the billions, obviously.

This is what makes Eastman a player to count on, and why I believe the longer-term AEPS growth (2028-2032) may be currently considered conservative.

Using my estimate of about 13x P/E, I therefore see a 15%+ annualized rate of upside for the company at a price of below $104/share and would consider Eastman to qualify as a "BUY" here, even 2 years after the fact. It should be noted, though, that this is an investment that may take time to fully "mature", and therefore should be entered with this in mind.

Thesis

Eastman Chemical Company remains a very qualitative business with an upside going into the upcoming years. It also now comes at a superb valuation. It works to become completely ESG-neutral by 2050, and its pathway to the growth it is seeking has been mostly confirmed since 2026, despite problematic legacy end markets. I increase my P/E average target for the company to about 13x P/E, which comes to a PT of about $104/share because investing at that level, we can see 15% annualized towards that 13x P/E on a long-term basis, assuming the growth rates hold.

Risks for the company are limited to legacy end market demand, which is volatile, and execution for its ESG and recycling targets and goals, which may go up and down a bit, but I view this investment as one for the longer term. I may increase my position here going forward - though at my IG, Wolf of Value, I cover other companies with upsides that I consider either equal or better.

As of 2026, I am reiterating "BUY" for Eastman Chemicals.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them.

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this and a price target of $104/share, where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Buy" rating as of April 2026. The company fulfills almost all of my fundamental criteria for investment.

data.title: Industry Outlook: Visible Alpha Breakdown Of U.S. Banks' First Quarter 2026 Earnings Expectations
data.description:
US large-cap banks kick off first quarter earnings season on Tuesday, April 14, with JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), Citigroup Inc. (C), and Wells Fargo & Co. (WFC) reporting first, followed by Bank of America Corp. (BAC) on Wednesday.

The big four entered 2026 with strong momentum following a record 2025, supported by resilient economic activity, moderating inflation, and still-elevated interest rates. Markets have remained constructive but volatile, with rate-cut expectations shifting and capital markets activity gradually improving from late-2025 lows. Against this backdrop, Visible Alpha consensus expectations point to steady top-line growth, modest profitability gains, and early signs of normalization in credit costs.

Across the group, revenues are expected to grow in the mid-to-high single digits year-over-year, led by JPMorgan (+7.3%), Citi (+8.3%), Wells Fargo (+7.5%), and Bank of America (+5.7%). This growth is driven by continued strength in trading and investment banking pipelines, which began recovering in Q4 2025, stable loan growth, particularly in consumer and commercial segments, and elevated (though plateauing) net interest income (NII) levels.

Profitability is expected to improve, with EPS growth ranging from 7% at JPMorgan to over 30% at Citi, the latter benefiting from restructuring progress and operating leverage. ROE trends are expected to be mixed, improving at JPMorgan, Bank of America, and Citi, but declining at Wells Fargo. While efficiency ratios show improvement at JPMorgan and Bank of America and deterioration at Citi and Wells Fargo, reflecting ongoing investment spend and restructuring costs.

Net interest margins face incremental pressure

Despite solid revenue growth, net interest margins (NIMs) are expected to show mixed-to-declining trends. Bank of America, Citi, and Wells Fargo are all expected to see sequential NIM compression, while JPMorgan stands out with a modest NIM uptick. Banks have flagged that the peak benefit from higher rates is behind them, and Q1 expectations reinforce that trend. Still, NII remains resilient, growing across all banks.

Balance sheet growth remains intact

Loan and deposit growth is expected to remain broadly positive in Q1. Total assets, deposits, and loans are seen increasing across banks. The most notable trend into Q1 is continued strength in loan growth. This builds on Q4 momentum, where banks pointed to healthy consumer balance sheets and steady borrowing activity, alongside improving corporate demand tied to a rebound in dealmaking and investment banking pipelines.

Stable fundamentals support cautious normalization

A key area of focus this quarter will be credit normalization. On an aggregate level, provisions for loan losses are expected to decline modestly, driven primarily by reductions at JPMorgan (-7.5% YOY) and Citi (-4%), partially offset by a notable increase at Wells Fargo (+22.6%) and a broadly flat trend at Bank of America (+0.8%).

Importantly, non-performing asset (NPA) ratios remain low, generally below 1%. So, while some banks are building reserves cautiously, actual credit deterioration remains contained.

Capital ratios show a mixed picture. JPMorgan and Citi are expected to strengthen CET1 ratios in Q1, while Bank of America and Wells Fargo are expected to see slight declines. This comes amid continued uncertainty around Basel III endgame regulations, which banks have been preparing for since late 2025. Capital return expectations (buybacks/dividends) will remain a key discussion point on earnings calls.

Overall, Q1 2026 is expected to shape up to be a solid quarter for large US banks. Revenue growth is projected to remain supported by resilient lending activity and improving market conditions, while margin pressures and elevated costs continue to weigh on operating leverage. At the same time, credit quality is seen remaining stable and capital levels strong, providing a solid foundation as banks navigate an evolving macro and regulatory environment.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: THD: Thailand Stocks Defy Bearish News In Early 2026, But Too Early To Buy The Dip
data.description:
iShares MSCI Thailand ETF overview

The iShares MSCI Thailand ETF (THD) is a very broad-based ETF offering exposure to Thailand’s stock market. It has a long history dating back to 2008; however, it has produced poor performance. According to its latest factsheet, it held over 80 different securities, with only one stock having a weight of over 10% of the portfolio. The top ten securities account for 57% of the portfolio, and the largest sector weight is currently the 14% exposure to the energy sector.

I last covered THD in August of 2024, which was, as we have become accustomed to, an interesting time in Thai politics. Back then I did see some green shoots in terms of how such political moves may potentially affect their stock market. I also viewed the historic poor returns as providing a cheap valuation backdrop where a contrarian buying opportunity may eventuate.

Since that time, almost a couple of years ago now, Thai stocks have been very volatile. In the context of even more eventful times in Thai politics over the last year, I thought it was an opportune time to take another look at their stock market.

I still find the valuation story and the contrarian aspect to owning Thailand stocks now tempting. The news flow is full of more gloom than previously in terms of the outlook for the Thai economy and stock market. At the same time, though, dips in their market have been getting shallower. This setup can often be a bullish one, as it indicates perhaps any potential sellers of Thailand equities have had enough reasons to act already.

iShares MSCI Thailand ETF key facts

Are Thai stocks climbing a wall of worry?

In late 2024, I referred to various Thailand economic policy discussions that were an encouraging sign the country was open-minded to actively boost economic growth.

In this respect, 2025 was generally plagued by ongoing uncertainty on many of these initiatives. Such uncertainty now, though, is increasingly looking to now be in the rearview mirror.

Some examples include:

  • Thailand’s “Digital Wallet” scheme—This cash handout scheme was widely criticized by economists. Some cynics viewed it as primarily a data-capturing exercise. It began with moderate impact and shortly after was shelved so the funding could instead be directed to other areas.
  • Government measures to boost the stock market—Significant money was raised for a new Thai ESG fund launched in April last year by offering tax incentives. This followed the success of the Vayupak Fund, a Thai state fund that raised US$4.5 billion and helped stabilize their equity market in late 2024.
  • Visa reform to boost tourism—In July of 2024, Thailand announced longer visa stays to boost their economy. At the time there were debates about whether such measures were too relaxed. It was not long before these changes were linked to more issues with mule bank accounts and a crackdown involving bank account freezes. Since then, the new Destination Thailand Visa (DTV) has been scrutinized far more heavily. The more relaxed 60-day visa-free stay for many tourists is now set to revert to the old 30-day visa-free period.
  • Thailand joining BRICS—While economists also debated the negative impacts of Thailand joining BRICS, the country was able to navigate the related worries about its trade relationship with the U.S. After the Thailand stock market initially fell heavily on news of new tariff measures threatened by the Trump administration on “Liberation Day,” negotiations were quick to take place. Thailand, along with most of Southeast Asia, soon finalized new lower tariff rates.

If there is one thing stock markets generally hate, it is any sort of uncertainty. Putting aside one's views on the effectiveness of various measures discussed above, at least there looks to be a clearer path forward on such policies.

Recent Thailand election stock market implications

Another major uncertainty Thailand had to deal with recently is the election held in February this year. This was particularly weighing on the minds of stock market investors, given Thailand's political history involving instability and regular military coups.

The Thailand 2026 election result came as a surprise. Looking at it purely from a stock market investor’s perspective, the result is positive. It provides a more certain path forward for various reasons.

To start with, it was a clear win that avoided the potential for the result to be debated for weeks or months into the future. The result suggested Thailand prioritized stability over reform. While one could argue some major economic reform is necessary, a new government tackling such reform is often something stock market investors would prefer to steer clear of. Investors typically rather adopt a “better the devil you know” mindset when it comes to government intervention.

To illustrate the above, without wanting to read too much into short-term stock market sentiment, given the surprise election result, it is interesting to observe what occurred after that weekend. The Monday after the election result, Thai markets surged by 3.5 percent. – “In a sign of optimism, global funds bought the most Thai stocks in four years on Monday (Feb 9)”.

Thailand's tourism sector outlook is admittedly poor

One key concern is the worrying outlook for Thailand’s tourism sector, which can’t seem to take a trick right now over the last year or two. Various factors out of left field have not helped.

Early in 2025, a Chinese actor was abducted from Thailand, and the publicity this generated was significant. To quote from the previously linked article, “The publicity around Wang’s case couldn’t have come at a worse time for Thai tourist operators, who had been expecting a surge of arrivals during the Lunar New Year holiday, China’s peak travel season. But there are signs Chinese tourists are looking elsewhere. Over the weekend, flight cancellations from China to Thailand surged 150%, Chinese state media reported, citing data from travel analytics firm ForwardKeys.”

As 2025 progressed, the relaxed attitude on the changes to visas announced in mid-2024 seemed to have changed. The media was quick to jump on the possible links between such relaxed visa policies and other matters. A potential connection was suggested to abductions and crime syndicates, as mentioned in the previous article, and the crackdown I touched on earlier with freezing many Thai bank accounts.

Adding to the headwinds for the tourism sector was a stronger Thai baht than most had expected. This occurred whilst there were mixed messages about how committed Thailand was to visas like the DTV and the recent visa-free period expansion to 60 days for many countries.

We also had major escalations in tensions between Thailand and Cambodia. This saw widespread deadly clashes along the border, particularly worrying during July of 2025 and again later that year in December.

Now tourism is interrupted by the Iran war. Dubai is a common transit hub for tourists entering Thailand that holidaymakers would now want to avoid. Then there is the obvious fact of elevated airfares and other cost inflation issues as a result of this conflict.

As I write this, it is days away from the Thailand New Year, and what typically follows is “low season” for tourism. This is why I expect no relief over the next few months at least regarding the news flow surrounding Thailand’s bleak tourism sector prospects.

That being said, as I suggested earlier, many of these issues above could be considered “left field” in nature. The fundamental attractions of Thailand as a tourist destination have not disappeared. In fact, maybe in the medium term it can appeal to the Dubai social media influencer types who might now be reconsidering where to make their home base.

The likely short-term negative news flow in upcoming months could pave the way for a buying opportunity in Thai stocks.

Thai stocks significantly underperformed in 2025

Emerging market equities in general had a very good 2025, finally outperforming the U.S. One notable exception, however, was Thailand (+4%). Their stock market also performed very poorly last year versus some regional peer ETFs such as Malaysia (+17%), Vietnam (+65%), and Singapore (32%).

iShares MSCI Thailand ETF long term performance

The long-term performance has been very disappointing, although momentum has shifted in the last 12-month period.

Thailand stock market valuations in 2026 after Q1

The underperformance of Thai stocks in 2025 depressed valuations to such an extent that it has assisted in providing a backdrop of strong gains thus far in 2026. In Q1 the Stock Exchange of Thailand Index, SET50, gained almost 15%.

Despite this very strong start in 2026, Thailand still screened as the 6th cheapest market in a list of 43 countries in the world, according to work from Meb Faber's The Idea Farm, as shown here.

One can refer to the previous link to see how this analysis is based off quarterly CAPE ratio updates as of March 31, 2026. It also combines a range of other quantitative valuation variables.

It is worth noting in the above context how the Thailand market might be disadvantaged in such comparisons to peers due to its lower weighting of financials. Bank stocks have a tendency to trade cheaply on measures such as P/E and P/B. This might bias other equity markets with high exposures to banks to look much cheaper. For example, in markets such as Indonesia, Singapore, and Malaysia, you will find their ETFs have circa 40 to 50% exposure to the financial sector. The Thailand ETF, in comparison, sits at just a 13% weight.

We now have a rare setup where the Thailand stock market appears to have both valuations and momentum in its favor.

iShares MSCI Thailand ETF Top Holdings and Sectors

iShares MSCI Thailand ETF portfolio characteristics

Weak economic outlook for Thailand in 2026, but do stock market investors care?

To be clear, Thailand is not the stock market for those that believe that strong economic growth is a prerequisite for a country's equities to produce good returns.

I would like to point out, though, that looking backwards at last year's GDP or the country's historical returns over the last decade is not always a good approach. An interesting reminder of this just last year in emerging markets was the performance of the stock markets of India vs. South Korea. Stocks did ok in India last year, rising around 10%, with their GDP growth sitting impressively around 7%. South Korea's GDP, on the other hand, was much lower at around 2%. Yet after their stock market performed poorly for many years, it finally surged circa 80% last year.

One factor helping South Korean stocks last year was a government initiative specifically aimed at helping their stock market known as the Value-Up Program. Hence it is why I felt it was worth mentioning earlier some recent government initiatives in Thailand, such as the Vayupak Fund and new Thai ESG funds.

It is helpful when a country prioritizes its stock market. Many investors also view the good recent run in Japanese equities as at least partially helped by their government introducing corporate reforms. Japan's GDP growth has been quite low the last few years, even as their stock market has surged.

One should fully expect that Thailand will record sluggish economic growth this year. According to the research from the Asia Development Bank shown here, Thailand is on course to record the slowest growth in ASEAN, with their forecast at just 1.8%.

Risks

In ADB's economic forecasts mentioned above, they highlight some factors I already discussed earlier. These prominently include mounting pressure from softer tourism and high energy costs as a fallout from the Middle East conflict.

I made the point earlier that such factors have the potential to be more temporary in nature and thus could provide a potential dip in Thai stocks to consider buying.

The bigger, longer-term risks I see with the Thailand stock market are more structural in nature. To quote directly from the ADB research, "ADB also pointed to the burden of household debt as a major domestic constraint. Private consumption has remained soft as families continue to face heavy debt obligations, limiting spending power at a time when higher energy and logistics costs are feeding through the economy."

Conclusion

I have approached my latest look at the Thailand stock market with a mindset that a buying opportunity may emerge. This is primarily due to cheap valuations and it feeling like one of the more "hated" markets I have seen in recent years. The news is pessimistic, yet such news is usually so widely known and discussed I wonder whether the selling of this market might have become exhausted last year.

I do not yet have any meaningful exposure to Thai equities, though, and it is possible this opportunity was more of one to act on late last year.

With the election result going the way it did, though, and the market pulling back from the highs a few weeks ago, I wanted to take a closer look again.

Due to the Iran conflict, though, it is anyone's guess where the Thailand SET Index might settle in even a week from now. Just as I was getting a little bit tempted to buy the dip after a 10% correction, as I write this on the weekend of April 11th, it has recovered much of this already.

I would therefore hold off buying THD for now. The news flow may well get worse before it gets any better. I do see it as encouraging, however, how the market bounced in mid-last year and was very resilient amidst negative headlines throughout. I therefore view any future potential correction to retest where we began the year at around the 1,250 level on the SET Index as a dip to buy. This likely equates to approximately the low $60 range for this THD ETF.

data.title: Haverty Furniture: Idiosyncratic, Cash Yield Income Play But Not Best-In-Class Value
data.description:
Haverty Furniture Companies, Inc. (HVT) is a specialty furniture retailer serving the suburban US market through 129 stores, so it has a mid-market footprint, reflective also of its mid-to-small cap market cap of around $370 million when considering class A into common stock conversions. They are also, therefore, still in a growth tenor, targeting 5 openings of stores, usually in big box real estate, per year in the Southeastern US in affluent markets. They try to do a more tailored model, giving free consultations to buyers, who then typically make significantly larger purchases (double), likely reflecting winning wallet share through that sort of in-store, almost interior design engagement. At this scale, it reflects an effective competitive lever. The normalized FCFY is close to 10%, we think, once 2026 expansion step-ups are passed, and also given the fact that tariffs may be firmly deemed illegal. Also, additional tariffs of 5% on wood furniture hadn't been put into place in time before the Supreme Court ruling. But in general, the company has mitigated tariff exposure thanks to a strong upholstery business where import intensity is lower, and also as demonstrated in the results where China exposure was reduced to below 5% and direct imports generally limited.

The strong FCFY, which we believe is the main draw here, easily supports the around 6% dividend yield currently and also continued growth, making Haverty an attractive income play. They have around $177 million in real estate, and net of the net financial position driven by leases, that covers around a third of the market cap. However, the real estate and value angle, in our view, remain sharper at our high-conviction furniture play, Leon's Furniture (LNF:CA), which also has FCFYs around 10% currently. So they win out. Moreover, there are clearly risks with respect to reinflation and how that could affect discretionary purchases on the front line as a consequence of demand destruction. Nonetheless, Haverty is a solid income play with a decent FCFY proposition. It's just not the optimal play within our coverage universe in our view.

Business and Results

They are proposing significantly higher CAPEX in 2026 compared to 2025, where cash flow run rates from operations have been between $50 million and $60 million in the last two years. FCF will be halved, it looks like, into 2026 in order to drive growth. Most of the FCF will be going into dividends, which look just about sustainable.

We're impressed by the cash flow generation. Even in 2026, which is slated to be a growth year and CAPEX heavy, FCFY is still going to be above 5%, it looks like, based on 2025 as a base year for the operating cash flow, which is mostly protected from deleterious tariff impacts. Normalized, it is closer to 8% FCFY and can be higher once coasting off established new locations and having grown as a consequence. This also means good coverage of the 6% dividend yield, which is looking pretty sustainable.

We note that they don't close stores too often, and there isn't a lot of turnover in the footprint, so it looks like they're generally pretty judicious with their store expansion, given that the company also remains solidly profitable with a 5.3% Q4 operating margin.

Florida and Texas are the biggest markets, and they own the real estate in Florida and Virginia. Regionally they are focused on the Southeastern US.

Only 3.2% of their sales are online, with purchases being made in store.

In terms of the latest results, sales and operating profits were up around 10% YoY in Q4, an acceleration from the more or less 5% sales growth and operating profit growth for the FY. A third of sales are financed by third parties, so Haverty doesn't carry these credit risks. The distribution network is impressive, with 3-5 average delivery days for items in stock and up to 7 on average for special order items.

Recovery in sales beginning post-COVID-19 has managed to produce GP growth this year. They didn't bulge inventories in early 2025, which indicates that they weren't benefiting from an inventory frontloading ahead of tariffs that would have only been speculated at the time (there aren't massive inventories as of March 31, 2025). Therefore, the GP reflects the higher purchasing costs of inventories, with all accumulated inventory before tariffs likely run through by a little bit beyond the half year. So we have a pretty good run rate of the post-tariff profitability just by looking at the 10-K results.

Upholstery is around 50% of sales. Direct upholstery imports are a pretty limited part of sales at 1.5%, compared to 7.3% for case goods. So the tariff exposure isn't of the worst type. Though 63% of the furniture that they sell was not produced domestically. But that is actually a lot lower import exposure compared to most furniture companies that might have been entirely exposed to imports. 5% of purchases or less are now being made from China, where tariffs are theoretically the highest.

Bottom Line

All financial obligations are leases, covered substantially by cash and therefore in a net balance of around $60 million. They have $80 million in a credit facility that is unused as of now. Leverage is not an issue here given around $45 million in EBITDA (1.5x NFP/EBITDA). They also have around $170 million in real estate value at book. It's not as good of a real estate angle as Leon's has, but it's solid enough additional value to be made very comfortable with an almost 10% normalized FCFY, also factoring in possible growth from new store openings planned for 2026 as well as possibly the loss of the tariff lever as imposed by the Trump administration following the Supreme Court ruling that the tariffs as instituted were not legal. Though the company in its guidance is assuming the tariffs continue through different legal vectors, though with gross profit guidance between 60.5% and 61%, worsening is not in the cards, unless, of course, volumes are hit by the Iran War reinflation risk, which could cause consumers to pare back discretionary spending like furniture.

The 10% FCFY supports the 6% dividend yield and also the almost 5% yield recently approved buyback program. It doesn't have the edge in terms of value over Leon, which has a major real estate angle and actually comparable FCFY to Haverty's normalized and forecast FCFY, but it's a decent income play and, being of smaller scale, has more scope for idiosyncratic growth that could even buck some of the broader consumer discretionary trends in the worst case that reinflation re-entrenches itself to the detriment of the consumer. Also, its tariff exposure is what we'd call less than average for furniture, where margins are thin and typically imports are even more critical for the business.

data.title: Waste Connections: Waiting For A Better Entry Point Amid Iran Inflation Risks
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered Waste Connections (WCN), I highlighted its strong position as one of North America's leading waste collection service providers, although their premium valuation limited the attractiveness of an entry.

Although the company’s foundation remains solid despite the higher one-time capital needed in 2025 and the stock getting more attractive now, WCN remains a hold, trading around its fair value if we demand what I consider to be an appropriate return from a company like this given the current environment.

Foundation Remains Strong

Despite missing the market’s EPS estimates and reporting revenue in line, WCN’s cash flow continued to grow as they expanded, with the adj. FCF reached ~$1.26 billion, for a decent 3.45% increase YoY. This was marked by a ~$200 million Chiquita Canyon headwind related to the closure and environmental remediation of the landfill, with this type of expense expected to reach between $100 million and $150 million in 2026.

However, for 2026, they’re expecting double-digit growth in adj. FCF in 2026 to $1.40 billion to $1.45 billion, for a strong 11.2% to 15.1% increase YoY, expecting a 5% to 5.5% increase in core prices and cost moderation compared to 2025 (especially labor inflation), with $1.25 billion in CAPEX that includes another $100 million for their renewable natural gas and recycling facilities - all of which comes on top of the accretion of their acquisitions, so the company is growing on several fronts.

Financially, based on WCN’s latest report, we see an overall fine position, with current assets not covering the current liabilities and a lot of goodwill as a result of the significant acquisitions made throughout their history. The company's leverage stood at 2.75x at the end of 2025 (vs. 2.67x at the end of 2024), within their 2.5x to 3.0x total debt to EBITDA ratio as per their revolving credit agreement, and we also see a recent $600 million note issuance coming at a 4.8% yield, due in March 2036, used to repay their near-term portion of their borrowings outstanding on the revolving credit facility.

This debt is spread very well into the future, coming at small interest rates and not maturing until all the way in 2028, which can buy them enough time before having to refinance at slightly higher rates, highlighting their solid position.

Alongside their 0.85% dividend following last year’s increase, representing about $355 million per year, we see WCN returning an additional $505.52 million in buybacks, which adds up to a yield of about 2.06%, which is not terrible for a ~60.37% yield based on the midpoint adj. FCF guidance.

WCN will report their Q1 earnings on April 22nd, with the market expecting EPS of $1.05 and $2.35 billion in revenue. I don’t expect some major swings unless they announce some kind of acquisition spree, but what I’ll pay close attention to is their tone (or even direct statements) regarding the economy, because waste can be a great indicator of how the economy is going, even though it may be too early to draw any conclusions for the Iran conflict since it escalated in March.

Defensive, But Not Immune

Although we’re talking about a very defensive company, we should keep in mind that defensive doesn’t mean immune, and I highlighted before for them and their peers how these companies are also experiencing slowdowns during times of market weakness. Ultimately, a weak economy also translates into issues for the company’s clients, lower consumption, fewer transactions, issues in discretionary budgets, etc., which can translate into less waste for them to collect alongside fewer businesses to serve.

Given the potential wave of inflation caused by the Iran conflict, we should see much more than just high pump prices, with the potential to see this spread into virtually everything given the vital role the region plays for oil, nitrogen-based fertilizers, LNG, etc., which can put pressure on several other vital industries. WCN’s CFO also mentioned this relationship during their Q4 Earnings Call:

So we saw some incremental weakness early in the fourth quarter, then there was stabilization and what we saw most recently was a little uptick in OCC, which was encouraging. (...) So then what we're watching for and looking forward to would really be the uptick, which is driven by underlying economic activity, which ultimately drives the demand, most importantly, for fiber, which as you'll recall is the majority of the value in a ton of recyclables. So cardboard, so commerce, right, demand, consumer confidence, all those things that are the engines of driving consumption, which ultimately is what drives our business and recycled commodity values.

As a result, central banks will have to watch this very carefully, and we know that rate hikes are one of their main tools to fight rising inflation, which can be terrible for the stock market.

However, that doesn’t mean this has to be a bad thing for WCN as a company. Sure, it’s a slowdown, and nobody likes that (potential pressure for the stock), but the company’s management has a great track record for running and expanding the business well, and I wouldn’t be surprised to see them take advantage of such an environment to buy smaller peers in their very fragmented market at even better prices.

I’ll note again however that I’d like to see the company tap into their equity (i.e. issue stocks) to fund this expansion, especially since the leverage is approaching the high end of their target, as this is a major advantage over private peers, being able to issue at arguably high valuation metrics - even if they’re cheaper compared to larger peers. The company’s CEO also replied to a question regarding M&A and a potential “outsized year” in this regard, stating:

Now how much of an outsized relative to a normal $150 million to $200 million year? The year needs to play out to see that. But I think hopefully, you look back at the last 3 years' track record, and we're not seeing something that would make us think that this year looks differently. And we certainly have the capacity, as we said in our script, to do both whatever comes along at M&A and as much buyback and return of capital as we think is prudent based on the fundamentals of our business and what is driving those opportunities in the buyback. So we don't see any limitations on any of those.

Especially if rates remain high for longer (or worse), issuing debt can be quite costly for them, which can spiral into higher borrowing costs as their refinancing would be more expensive and the credit rating would risk dropping - although I believe that’s far from them right now. Their recent notes pricing is a good example here, with the $600 million they issued coming at a 4.8% yield, which is by no means a terrible yield, but it’s more than twice their lowest (2.2%) coupon.

Risks Reiterated

Although WCN and its industry have been solid in recent years and the business has been growing well, we can already see some pressure on their stocks from the Iran conflict, which can be just the beginning.

Since they are ultimately tied to the economy, we should also keep in mind that the past few years were just a natural slowdown caused by rate hikes, while the current macro environment can cause significantly worse levels of pressure, which would likely be reflected in the stock price despite the company’s performance.

Valuation

For WCN's DCF to equity valuation, starting from their $1.425 billion estimated adj. FCF in 2026 following the normalization of the factors named above, with no net borrowings to highlight their pure cash generation potential. I'll assume a 10% CAGR over the first 5 years to take into account the potential macro improvements, M&A activity (with potential increases in leverage being reduced afterwards, as they usually do), including here the effects of buybacks, which is not far from the market’s estimates.

This would be followed by a 5% CAGR as the company can continue expanding well in the longer term given the fragmented market and rural focus, with a 3% terminal growth rate to take into account their pricing power and vital services, with an 8.5% discount rate to take into account the macro risks and demand a solid margin of safety, with 256.47 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $38.61 billion, for an intrinsic value of $150.55 per share, which is very close to the current $161.59.

Conclusions

WCN remains a hold, trading around its fair value if we demand what I consider to be a good return in this market from a company like this.

There are risks to remember given the exposure to the environment and the Iran conflict’s implications on the economy, and I believe it’s important to buy this when the margin of safety is good enough.

data.title: IDVO: Built Different, Built Better For What's Next And Beyond
data.description:
The Amplify CWP International Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (IDVO) was one of my strongest recommendations last year, and it is good to see the ETF deliver on all fronts since then. IDVO shows a total return of ~30% at a time when the S&P 500 has delivered only ~10%. More importantly, IDVO has also lived up to its potential through lower volatility, benefiting from a decent value and dividend-focused diversification (not high-growth bets alone) and international exposure. The income promise has also been delivered alongside growth - at over 5% yields (TTM basis at current prices), IDVO does use call writing as an add-on rather than a core strategy to augment a more realistic income without hurting growth or NAV stability.

This analysis walks through critical and material changes in the IDVO portfolio (from July last year), with greater exposure to defensives, real assets, commodity-linked geographies, and a reduction in long-duration tech and financials. The portfolio and option strategy together look poised for the forward market, which is likely to be volatile and range-bound. IDVO retains a Strong Buy rating.

Portfolio Advantage

I downloaded the latest full holdings from the IDVO website and found significant changes to the portfolio compared to where IDVO was in July last year (at the time of my earlier thesis). The active management is clearly at play here.

Interestingly, IDVO has delivered a staggering ~30% total returns with a low starting point in tech (when markets were rallying around AI themes) and has been meaningfully downsized further (from ~12.75% earlier to ~8% now). Real assets exposure has stepped up - we see a far higher materials exposure now. Energy exposure has also gone up, although not as visibly as materials. The shift to real assets shows up in the top 10 holdings too (Nutrien, Agnico Eagle, and Cameco added). This move from tech to real assets also positions it better for a regime ahead when stickier inflation or stagflation risks start appearing.

Another thematic change in IDVO's portfolio is a strengthening of the defensive layer, with more allocation now in staples. Healthcare allocation is marginally up too, while utilities exposure remains stable. I expect this positioning to work out well as markets remain range-bound over the next few quarters, looking for a trigger for the next leg of rerating. There is a lack of urgent catalysts for rerating overall at the moment. In fact, this consolidation could go through phases of volatility, like we saw in the past month or so. Which makes IDVO's stronger defense a better way to focus on income while not sacrificing growth a lot.

Financial exposure has also been cut down significantly, but geographic spread has improved. We now have Bank of Montreal (BMO) and Sumitomo Mitsui (SMFG) in the top 10, broadening the rate cycle exposure to commodity-linked economies like Canada. In fact, the overall higher weight to Canada across the full portfolio compared to July 2025 (commodity-linked, as said before) positions the portfolio well for a stagflation environment. The reduced financial exposure overall also aligns well with the uncertain outlook ahead in terms of how higher-for-longer rates balance credit quality deterioration, especially if growth remains subdued.

Material geographic exposure changes, other than Canada overweight, are seen in US exposure reducing further (that is, lower expensive growth concerns) and lower emerging market exposure (lower China and Brazil weights), reducing geopolitical and currency risks, and lowering the overall growth stance in the international portfolio.

Overall, the portfolio changes look well aligned for the regime ahead. There is a lower growth stance (more defensive and less exposure to emerging markets) that prepares the portfolio for any corrections. However, the value and international diversification as a theme are not compromised, just realigned. This portfolio should work out well in flat or correcting markets and in an inflationary environment in the US.

The Option Layer

I like the option strategy in IDVO because it is extremely conservative - an add-on to the dividends already generated by its portfolio. The overall yields do not have a high ask from the option layer too, which helps position call writing less aggressively. The net result is far lower income from premiums, but more consistent and reliable.

The less aggressive option writing stance starts from the fact that only 15-20 names (as deduced from the full holdings downloaded from the ETF website as of April 11th, 2026) at the moment (from a portfolio of ~57 stocks) have an option layer. The common theme of stocks where option writing is seen is if they are large and liquid stocks (TSM, ASML, etc.), commodity plays (CCJ, WPM, etc.), cyclicals (RIO, MT), and defensives (AZN, NVS). This is selective and discretionary overwriting.

Secondly, the strategy does not aim to maximize premium capture. For instance, I could see some calls written for TSM at up to ~$450 strikes, which are far OTM (by ~20%) compared to current spot levels. The strike position is dynamic, across expiries and laddered at times, and seems to depend on the management's outlook for the stocks and sectors. On average, core growth names like TSM and ASML have calls written on far OTM strikes, allowing for sharp rallies to a large extent. For commodity or income bets, the strikes could be nearer to ATM strikes.

The option writing stance also ensures that only a part of the underlying holding is covered. In the case of TSM, for example, the portfolio holds 127k shares, while only 300 call contracts are written (~30k share equivalent). That is ~23% of overwriting activity. Similar levels of overwriting were observed for some other holdings too, like NTR, BMO, and CCJ (some of the material holdings I checked).

This selective and passive option writing does not mean a great boost for income. I have been advocating for higher option writing as the route to more income and hence higher total returns in the case of several covered call strategies I have reviewed recently, because the outlook for the broader markets is one of volatile but range-bound conditions, when heavy option writing works well. IDVO does not need to do that. It retains upside capture, aims for a lower income payout it can sustain, and positions the portfolio (as discussed in the previous section) for a flat or range-bound market rather than aggressively loading up on option writing. A well-balanced setup, in my view, for the upcoming regime, and it keeps several possibilities open if the market reading goes wrong.

Correlations, Drawdowns, and Dividends

One of the factors that I monitor for IDVO is whether it is sacrificing its dividend-focused portfolio positioning for growth (possible, especially in 2025-like growth markets). We have already discussed how the portfolio has turned more ready for a flat or correcting market, so that should answer concerns around diluted income from dividends of the underlying stocks. High-yield names like Enbridge, TotalEnergies, BMO, Vodafone, and BTI command significant weights in the current portfolio, showing that dividend emphasis is intact. The decrease in financial exposure is offset by an increasing exposure in energy, telecom, and staples. Growth exposure is not very high but exists - TSM and ASML together account for less than 7% of the portfolio and additionally have calls written on top to compensate for their lower yields.

Correlations have also started to meaningfully diverge more often now (compared to the US benchmark - S&P500), especially when markets have turned volatile. This was seen in the tariff sell-off in April last year and has again started to show up during times of geopolitical uncertainties.

The dropping correlation in stress regimes is not always a good thing for IDVO, and investors should keep in mind that the defensive and low-growth positioning does not mean IDVO drawdowns are minimal always. During the April 2025 sell-off, IDVO stood its ground better, with its commodity and non-US exposure helping decouple its trajectory positively. The same defensive and diversified characteristics did not show in the recent geopolitical risk-off period in March 2026. Liquidity-led exits meant IDVO saw even sharper drawdowns at one point due to its geographic diversification (emerging markets hit more by oil price uncertainties, for example) and cyclicals and materials exposure. This does not negate IDVO's defensive and lower volatility positioning. Over cycles, you will observe better risk-adjusted returns here, with income intact. In late 2025, just a few months of slowdown in growth markets was enough to allow IDVO to overtake the broader markets. That shows that IDVO's balance of growth and defense is working even after what was a far better environment for the S&P 500 in 2025 overall. The recovery post-ceasefire in Iran has also been quite strong.

I would say IDVO continues to be a strong buy as both a core portfolio allocator as well as an income vehicle. It continues to combine the best of many worlds, from total returns to income to sustainable NAVs. The positioning of the portfolio is such that it should continue to do well in an environment of stagflation or lower growth. It can also double up as a relatively defensive portfolio, unless March 2026-like liquidity-driven risk-off markets see corrections focused on materials, cyclicals, and specific geographies.

data.title: Week Ahead: Hope Springs Eternal
data.description:
Hope that the ceasefire will lead to the end of the Middle East war fanned risk appetites in recent days. Equities rallied broadly. May WTI and June Brent fell by nearly 11.5%. The dollar fell against all the G10 currencies, the Antipodeans, and the Scandis, which seem the most sensitive to growth prospects and the risk environment. The JP Morgan Emerging Market Currency Index rose by a little more than 2% to post its best week since March 2018.

Negotiations will take place over the weekend, and market participants are hopeful that this can lead to a more sustained end to hostilities. Of course, it is complicated by the lack of trust and the Israel-Lebanon conflict. Ceasefires are hardly immaculate. Meanwhile, we may be on the cusp of two other watersheds. First, there have been encouraging reports about a potential agreement between Russia and Ukraine. Second, Hungary’s Viktor Orban is the longest-serving prime minister in the EU. He has served for the past 16 years and had a previous term between 1998 and 2002. Polls suggest he could lose the popular vote on April 12.

US

Drivers: The two-week ceasefire in the Middle East war reset the dollar and interest rate expectations. The dollar rallied during the war and was sold on the ceasefire. The derivatives market sees the Federal Reserve as the most dovish of the major central banks, and the odds of a cut this year have risen to around 33% from briefly discounting a small chance of a hike.

Data: The US has a full slate of high-frequency reports in the coming days. We suspect most will have limited impact on the markets. Yet, given the desire for real-time insight into the impact of the war, the Empire State survey and the Philadelphia Fed's survey may be the most important. The Fed's Beige Book and its anecdotal reports may also serve this purpose, though practically no one doubts that the FOMC will stand pat when it meets later this month.

Prices: The Dollar Index (DXY) gapped lower in the middle of last week. That gap, found between about 99.18 and 99.51, is important from a technical perspective. It found support around the 200-day moving average, found near 98.50. The momentum indicators have turned down, and the five-day moving average has fallen below the 20-day moving average for the first time since February 18. We look for the 97.50 area in the coming days.

EMU

Drivers: The euro bottomed in the middle of March, but the upside seemed stalled until the ceasefire was announced. Expectations for ECB rate hikes this year were pared from at least three hikes to two, with about a 40% chance of a third. The US two-year premium over Germany was around 138 bp before the war, and it collapsed to about 108 bp as President Trump threatened to destroy Iran. The ceasefire announcement saw it recover to almost 130 bp and it finished last week near 123 bp.

Data: With several large members already reporting national figures for February industrial output (Germany 0.5%, France -0.7%, Spain 0.2%) the aggregate figure will likely not draw much attention. The same could be said about the aggregate trade and current account figures. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey of 0.3% growth in Q1 and Q2 this year seems a little exaggerated. Last month ECB projections saw growth at 0.9% this year and the current account surplus at 1.7% of GDP (1.9% in 2025), with a larger budget deficit (3.4% of GDP vs. 3.0% in 2025).

Prices: The euro appears to have forged a solid base in the second half of March. The consolidation of the past couple of sessions looks constructive. The five-day moving average is above the 20-day moving average, and the momentum indicators give the single currency scope for additional near-term gains. Nearby resistance is seen around $1.1745-50, but we suspect there may be initial potential toward $1.1825, the high from the last day in February, before the war began. Potential for a breakthrough in negotiations between Russia and Ukraine and a defeat of Hungary's Orban would seem supportive of the euro.

PRC

Drivers: Beijing continues to endorse the appreciation of the yuan. Last week, the PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at its lowest level in three years. The fix was lowered last week by about 0.30%, the largest weekly decline in two months.

Data: By the end of the week ahead, we will have been told Beijing's estimate of Q1 GDP and the macro details for March, including trade, retail sales, industrial production, investment, and house prices. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey is that the economy grew at a slightly faster pace in Q1 than it did in Q4 25 (1.2%).

Prices: Ahead of the weekend, the dollar posted its lowest close against the offshore yuan since March 2023. A break of CNH6.8200 could signal a move toward CNH6.80, but there is potential toward the 2023 low, which was slightly below CNH6.70. It is difficult to know the intent of Chinese officials, but they have embraced the gradual appreciation of the yuan. We suspect a move to CNY6.60-CNY6.70 may be acceptable.

Japan

Drivers: The 30-day correlation between changes in the dollar-yen exchange rate and the 10-year US yield continues to hover above 0.60, the highest since last September. It bottomed in early February a little below 0.15, which was the lowest since last May. The 30-day correlation of changes in the dollar-yen exchange rate and the Dollar Index is a little above 0.75. At the end of February, it was near 0.73 but fell below 60 in mid-March. It has not been above 0.80 since last October.

Data: After a 4.3% month-over-month surge in Japan's industrial production in January, a monster of a gain, the largest since June 2022, it slowed by almost half in February. It is subject to revision in the week ahead. While the Japanese economy seems sufficiently strong and price pressures sufficiently persistent for the Bank of Japan to hike later this month, one more worrisome development has been the weakness in private sector machinery orders (capex). They fell by 5.5% in January. The February report is due on April 15.

Prices: The market tested the JPY160 level early last week, and the news of the ceasefire saw the dollar come off to support near JPY158.00. The market does not appear to have given up on probing for the pain threshold of Japanese officials, though the daily momentum indicators are falling and the five-day moving average in threatening to fall through the 20-day moving average for the first time since February 20. That said, a convincing break of JPY157.50 would suggest a high is in place.

UK

Drivers: Sterling continues to trade inversely to the Dollar Index. The 30-day rolling correlation of the changes of the two instruments is almost -0.87. It has not been much more extreme than this since early Q4 25. The correlation between the exchange rate and changes in two-year US and UK yields stands around -0.35 and -0.10, respectively. Meanwhile, the correlation between changes in sterling and the US S&P 500 (risk proxy) is a little below 0.57. This is the upper end of the range since late 2022.

Data: At the end of the week, the UK reports February GDP and details. Even though the monthly figures do not translate into quarterly reports as one might intuitively expect, the market is often sensitive to them. January GDP unexpectedly stagnated (median forecast in Bloomberg's survey was 0.2%). Another disappointment would likely spark sterling sales and a reconsideration of the likely trajectory of BOE. The central bank projects 0.9% growth this year and the Office for Budget Responsibility forecasts 1.1%. This may also prove to be optimistic.

Prices: Ahead of the weekend, sterling settled at its best level since the war began. Yet it did not make it above the intraday March highs (~$1.3480-85). The momentum indicators are constructive, and the five-day moving average pushed above the 20-day moving average. Above the March highs is the $1.3515 area, which is the halfway mark of the losses since the January 27 four-year high was reached (~$1.3670). A note of caution is that the upper Bollinger Band is slightly below $1.3500.

Canada

Drivers: Around mid-March, the 30-day correlation of changes in the US dollar-Canadian dollar exchange rate and the Dollar Index rose above 0.85, its highest since mid-2024. It is now near 0.65, which is still at the upper end of last year's range. The correlation between changes in the exchange rate and the S&P 500 is around -0.28, while the correlation with changes in the US two-year yield is slightly lower, though near the highs since last October.

Data: Canada housing data does not capture the market's attention, but the international transactions may be a somewhat different matter. After a weak H1 25, foreign demand for Canada's stocks and bonds picked up in H2 25 and continued into January. In fact, January's portfolio capital inflows were more than twice the Q4 25 average. On the other hand, the trade balance may be deteriorating. The Jan-Feb deficit of almost C$10 bln is among the largest Canada has ever recorded, and the rolling 12-month average shortfall of C$3.675 bln is a record.

Prices: Before the weekend, and despite a milquetoast jobs report that saw full-time jobs lost for the second consecutive month, the Canadian dollar rose to its best level in nearly two and a half weeks. The greenback was pushed below CAD1.38 and settled below the 20- and 200-day moving averages, which converged around CAD1.3820. The momentum indicators have turned down. The next target is around CAD1.3750, and then possibly CAD1.37.

Australia

Drivers: Changes in the Australian dollar and the Dollar Index are the most inversely correlated (30 days) since last October (~-0.78). The correlation between the exchange rate and the S&P 600 is around 0.64, which is off the peak seen last month near 0.73. Last year's high was closer to 0.87. The Aussie's correlation with gold peaked in February a little above 0.80. It fell to almost 0.30 in March before recovering to almost 0.50 now.

Data: At the end of the week, Australia reports March jobs data. In February, the almost 49k increase in employment was fueled exclusively by part-time positions. There were 30.5k fewer full-time jobs. However, that came after two solid months in which full-time posts rose by nearly 110k. The unemployment rate is 4.3%. It has spent only one month higher since the end of 2021, and that was the anomalous jump to 4.5% last September and a return to 4.3% in October. The Reserve Bank of Australia meets next on May 5. The futures market has about a 65% chance of a hike. We wonder if three hikes in a row are a bit aggressive given the hornet's nest that has been opened by the Middle East war.

Prices: The Australian dollar is knocking on the $0.7100 area, which it last traded above on March 19. Following the second rate hike of the year, the Aussie reached slightly beyond $0.7185 on March 13, its best level since mid-2022. The momentum indicators have turned higher from oversold, and the five-day moving average crossed back above the 20-day moving average last week. Initial resistance above $0.7100 is seen near $0.7125.

Mexico

Drivers: The Mexican peso is sensitive to the US dollar's broad direction. The rolling 30-day correlation of changes in the USD-peso exchange rate and the Dollar Index is above 0.75 for the first time since last October, which also marked the high in 2025. Yet the peso is also sensitive to the risk environment. Using the S&P 500 as the proxy, the USD-peso exchange rate is inversely correlated to changes in the S&P 500 by almost 0.80, the most extreme since July 2020. The correlation between changes in the exchange rate and US two-year yields is near 0.45, the highest since last September.

Data: While keeping in mind the large informal economy in Mexico, which accounts for a little over half of the workforce according to some estimates, nominal wage increases have been trending lower. The March figures are due this week. The rolling six-month moving average was slightly above 6.35% in February, the smallest since Q1 22.

Prices: The Mexican peso appreciated by about 3.3% against the US dollar last week. That is its best weekly showing since September 2024. The dollar settled near MXN17.2270 on the eve of the Middle East war and peaked in late March around MXN18.1645, slightly shy of the 200-day moving average. At the end of last week, it tested the MXN17.25 area, the lowest since March 2. The momentum indicators are falling, and the five-day moving average fell below the 20-day moving average last week for the first time since early March. However, the move has taken place so quickly that the greenback has settled for the past three sessions below the lower Bollinger Band (~MXN17.36 before the weekend).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Strattec: Profitability Transformation Is Driving The Story
data.description:
Strattec Security Corporation (STRT) is a stock that has continued to perform well since I first reviewed it. There have been some fluctuations but overall the stock has maintained a positive trend line. That is really due to the continued delivery and performance from the company. It continues to grow and has also increased profitability. The company continues to be a stock that seems to fly under the radar but produces results.

Last time I reviewed the stock there were some concerns about the auto market in general slowing down. While this has been the case, the company still showed growth and improved performance. It was able to grow despite the industry overall slowing down.

The stock recently pulled back from its highs and is sitting at a point where I think there continues to be value. The company has guided for a slowdown in the second half of the year, but the company has put itself in a position to be profitable despite a slowdown. It has also built a cash position of strength.

Financials

The company has continued to perform well. It really has shown growth and expansion across the board on the financial metrics. This has come despite concerns about the overall market demand provided at year end guidance for 2026 the company has been able to grow revenues year over year.

The company reported sales of $137.5 million for Q2, growth of 6% from the prior year. The revenue growth came despite the market slowdown that the company noted during year end. It was able to make up for a slower market by getting better pricing, favorable mix on content, new program launches, and tariff recovery. This is a real win for the company. Despite industry headwinds and a slower market the company was able to still grow revenues in a declining overall market. The concern here is if that is sustainable going forward.

The real transformation for the company has been the margins. The company reported an increase in gross profit of $5.6 million from the prior year. Gross margin for Q2 was 16.5% up by by 3.3% from the prior year. That is a huge increase that passes down to the bottom line. Much of this increase is due to pricing increases and product mix, but a big part of it is due to the restructuring and savings the company has been able to implement. This should carry forward and is not a one time bump. This can be seen as it has been 4 straight quarters that the gross margin has been over 16%. It does appear the gross margin gains are slowing down or stalling out. The company had a gross margin of 16.7% in Q4 and 17.3% in Q1. The latest quarter is down from the last 2 quarters. The company saw less headwind on the market side in Q1 which would contribute to higher sales and gross margin. Business will fluctuate and the margin is going to be affected by that, but I think there is a new higher baseline on the gross margin side of the business that will be maintained going forward.

This increase in margins passes through to overall profitability. Scale and leverage is starting to show up for the company in a significant way. Income from operations more than doubled from the prior year to reach $4.8 million, up from $2.1 million in the prior year. The first 6 months looks the same with an operating income of $15.3 million vs $7.2 million in the prior year. Adjusted EBITDA and Adj. EBITDA margin also increased significantly with the margin up by 2.8% over prior year. EPS nearly tripled to $1.20 from $0.32 in the prior year.

Lastly is the cash flow. The company reported cash from operations of $13.9 million up by $4.4 million, or 47% from the prior year. This has put the company in a position of strength on the balance sheet. It ended the quarter with $99 million in cash and equivalents, up from $90.5 million in Q1.

Restructuring and Business Transformation Costs

The company has been going through a restructure that has significantly improved margins and efficiency within the business. The leverage for the business can be seen when you look across the quarters. A couple extra million in revenue can push margins higher. A large part of this is due to the discipline that the company has taken in terms of its expenses. It runs an effective business to increase margins. A large part of this is due to the restructuring efforts that the company has taken over the past year.

The company announced their latest restructuring and cost initiative by offering an early retirement program and additional restructuring in their Mexico operations. It is expected to save $3.4 million annually from the changes. This should continue to help with the margins for the business, even with the expected slowdown in the second half of the year.

Selling, administrative, and engineering expenses have continued to become a larger percentage of revenues. This has been one concern of mine. In the latest quarter SAE expenses made up 13% of sales compared to 11.6% in the prior year. This has been creeping up for quite some time for the company. Management addressed this and said they expect this to be in the 10-11% range going forward. Without the one time voluntary retirement Q2 would have landed in that range during Q2.

One thing that I noticed around the restructuring is that the company has added an expense line, “Business Transformation Costs”. This expense line item has been growing rapidly and is starting to become impactful. The company also backs it out of adjusted EBITDA so the impact is not as noticeable. In the latest quarter this cost has almost reached $1 million dollars. What is the company spending $1 million on related to business transformation? There is a separate line item for “restructuring and similar charges”. The company notes in the 10-Q that they are going through a business transformation to “upgrade systems and processes, modernization of our support functions and a focus on productivity and efficiencies in our manufacturing operations”. This is a line item that is vague on details and is becoming a larger expense. I then read the press release, “Strattec Accelerates Strategic Transformation with New Brand Identity”. This made me think it is worse. The company is touting that it updated a website and how that “marks another milestone in Strattec’s transformation journey”. I have personally created and manage a couple of websites, the cost at this point in time is pretty small. I sure hope that $1 million is going a lot further than updating websites for a company that does not even sell to consumers. This seems like a consulting line item that is a bit out of control. This is not a big expense at the moment but it has been doubling each quarter. I will keep watching this item.

The other one that I noticed was the massive increase in SBC. It increased to $1.13 million in the latest quarter, an increase of more than 68% quarter over quarter. The company has been performing well and hitting milestones but that is quite an increase in SBC. I don’t want to see the gains for the company all being used up in SBC. This is also something I will be watching going forward.

Outlook

The company guided for a sales slowdown in the second half of the year, with an overall decline of 3 to 4 percent for the second half of the year. The company has been able to overcome the market slowdown in the first half of the year, but a big part of that was due to pricing benefits. The company will lap price increases implemented last year starting in Q3, so those benefits will start to disappear. This will make it much tougher for the company to continue to show growth despite the industry headwinds. That being said I do think the company will perform better than estimates, just as it did to start the year.

There is also an expected decrease in cash flow for the second half of the year. That is more related to some of the charges that will come with the early retirement plan. The company is also looking to build inventory a bit more. These are not big concerns. In the long run the savings from the early retirement package will pay off.

Management has positioned itself well for a market slowdown. The company has reduced costs and runs much more efficiently. Even if the market slows down the company can still generate a strong profit.

The one thing about the industry is that the company wins a contract for a vehicle and then it will provide the part for the vehicle for its life. That is typically 5-7 years before a company will remake the vehicle. This means it is really critical that Strattec get awarded during the design process to establish the continued business. The company has been focused on 2029 vehicle launches. It is critical that the company win those jobs now so that they are in line for the future production. Management brings it up each call and it is worth monitoring what management has to say about the sales cycle. Also look for any announcements around these wins. That establishes future revenue for the company.

The last thing to consider is the cash position. Management noted that this could be used to increase shareholder returns or also for future acquisitions. The company is not a high growth company but it is well established in its markets. I could easily see the company finding an acquisition to incorporate into its operations. This would provide growth for the company and utilize the growing cash.

Valuation

Strattec continues to carry a low valuation. The market cap sits at $343 million. It also has $99 million in cash with $2.5 million in debt. So the business itself is valued around $250 million. Trailing twelve month revenue was $586 million. That is a PS of .42 on EV/Sales. The industry average is 1.31. I understand that the business is not a high growth business. It does not necessarily deserve a premium valuation, but that is priced as if it is going to see a drop off in business. It should be noted that revenues are still expected to increase for the year as a whole.

That does not even take into account the growing profitability of the company. The company has been growing EPS by large percentages each quarter. The company trades at a PE of 12.47 well below the industry average of 20.4. From a valuation perspective it is very cheap compared to peers.

I do understand that sales are expected to slow in the second half of 2026. Also the company has a history of beating estimates. So I expect the slowdown to be less than what is guided. Overall for the year the company does expect to see an increase in revenues. Analysts do expect growth in revenues and earnings to slow down in the coming years, but regardless growth is expected along with continued profitability.

Conclusion

Strattec continues to perform. Despite there being industry headwinds the company grew revenues during the first half of the year. The real gain has come from the company restructuring its business and increasing profitability. The company has now reached a point where it is generating strong cash flow each quarter. It has also positioned itself for continued success by running a much more efficient business.

The company does not run at high growth rates and the growth is expected to slow down a bit more over the second half of the year. Despite this the company has been able to increase profits and cash flows. I would expect the company to continue to do the same going forward. The company has also built a strong cash position that can be utilized to increase shareholder return, or used for acquisitions and growth.
